# MSBuild Code Review: ContosoApp

## Repository Structure

| Path | SDK | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `src/ContosoApp.Core/` | `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` | Class library |
| `src/ContosoApp.Services/` | `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` | Class library |
| `src/ContosoApp.Api/` | `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web` | Web API |
| `test/ContosoApp.Core.Tests/` | `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` | xUnit test |
| `test/ContosoApp.Api.Tests/` | `Microsoft.NET.Sdk` | xUnit test |
| `Directory.Build.props` | — | ❌ **Missing** |
| `Directory.Build.targets` | — | ❌ **Missing** |
| `Directory.Packages.props` | — | ❌ **Missing** |

---

## 🔴 Errors (likely to cause problems)

### E1: Package version drift — same package at different versions

`Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions` is referenced at **two different versions** across projects:

| Project | Version |
|---|---|
| `ContosoApp.Core` | **8.0.0** |
| `ContosoApp.Services` | **8.0.1** |
| `ContosoApp.Api` | **8.0.0** |

And the test frameworks are at divergent versions:

| Package | `Api.Tests` | `Core.Tests` |
|---|---|---|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.9.0** | **17.10.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.7.1** | **2.8.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.5.6** | **2.8.1** |

**Why it matters:** Version drift means different projects get different behaviors, bug fixes, and API surfaces. At build time NuGet will pick one version for the whole dependency graph, but the _intent_ is unclear — was `8.0.1` a deliberate upgrade that missed two projects, or an accidental bump? This class of bug gets worse as the solution grows.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see **S1** below) — it eliminates this entire category of problem.

---

## 🟡 Warnings (anti-patterns that should be fixed)

### W1: Massive duplication — 8 identical properties copy-pasted across ALL 5 projects

Every single `.csproj` repeats this block verbatim:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<LangVersion>latest</LangVersion>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

**Why it matters:** When you need to change the target framework to `net9.0`, or update the company name, you must touch all 5 files and hope nobody misses one. As the solution grows, this becomes untenable. A `Directory.Build.props` file is the standard solution (see **S2**).

### W2: Duplicated `DocumentationFile` property across 3 src projects

All three `src/` projects contain an identical second `<PropertyGroup>`:
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

This should be centralized. It also belongs in the same PropertyGroup as the other properties — there's no reason for it to be a separate block.

### W3: Duplicated `PrintBuildInfo` target across 3 src projects

All three `src/` projects contain an identical target:
```xml
<Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
</Target>
```

This is a textbook case for `Directory.Build.targets`.

### W4: `LangVersion` set to `latest` is unnecessary for .NET 8

For SDK-style .NET 8 projects, the compiler automatically selects C# 12. Setting `LangVersion` to `latest` is equivalent to this default. More importantly, `latest` is a moving target — it silently changes meaning when you install a newer SDK. If you want the latest, the default is already that. If you want stability, pin to `12`.

**Recommendation:** Remove `LangVersion` entirely and rely on the SDK default, or pin to `12` if you want deterministic builds.

---

## 🔵 Suggestions (improvements for maintainability)

### S1: Adopt Central Package Management (CPM)

This is the **single highest-impact change** for this solution. Create a `Directory.Packages.props` at the repo root:

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Packages.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Shared runtime packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />

    <!-- Test packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.10.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="NSubstitute" Version="5.1.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

Then every `.csproj` drops the `Version=` attribute from its `<PackageReference>` items. For example, `ContosoApp.Core.csproj` would become:
```xml
<ItemGroup>
  <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" />
  <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" />
</ItemGroup>
```

**Benefits:** One place to update versions. Version drift becomes impossible. Upgrades via Dependabot/Renovate touch one file.

### S2: Create `Directory.Build.props` to centralize shared properties

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.props -->
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Documentation XML for src projects (test projects override IsPackable) -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Defaults for test projects -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

After this, **every `.csproj` shrinks dramatically**. For example, `ContosoApp.Core.csproj` becomes:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" />
    <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

And `ContosoApp.Core.Tests.csproj` becomes:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" />
    <PackageReference Include="NSubstitute" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\ContosoApp.Core\ContosoApp.Core.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

### S3: Create `Directory.Build.targets` for the shared build target

```xml
<!-- /Directory.Build.targets -->
<Project>
  <!-- Only for src projects, not tests -->
  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build"
          Condition="!$(MSBuildProjectName.EndsWith('.Tests'))">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>
</Project>
```

This goes in `.targets` (not `.props`) because targets should be defined after imports — that's the correct evaluation order.

### S4: Consider adding a `global.json` for SDK version pinning

There is no `global.json` in the repo. This means any developer's locally installed SDK version will be used, which can lead to "works on my machine" discrepancies.

```json
{
  "sdk": {
    "version": "8.0.400",
    "rollForward": "latestFeature"
  }
}
```

---

## 🟢 Positive (things done well)

- ✅ **All projects are SDK-style** — no legacy format migration needed
- ✅ **Consistent use of `TreatWarningsAsErrors`** across all projects — great for code quality
- ✅ **`Nullable` and `ImplicitUsings` enabled everywhere** — modern C# best practices
- ✅ **Test projects set `IsPackable` to `false`** — correct
- ✅ **No `packages.config`** — already using `PackageReference`
- ✅ **No stale `AssemblyInfo.cs` files** — relying on SDK auto-generation
- ✅ **Clean solution structure** with `src/` and `test/` separation

---

## Summary of Recommended Changes (Priority Order)

| # | Severity | Change | Files touched | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 🔴→🔵 | Create `Directory.Packages.props` (CPM) | 1 new + 5 edited | Eliminates version drift |
| 2 | 🟡→🔵 | Create `Directory.Build.props` | 1 new + 5 edited | Eliminates 40+ lines of duplication |
| 3 | 🟡→🔵 | Create `Directory.Build.targets` | 1 new + 3 edited | Eliminates 3 duplicated targets |
| 4 | 🟡 | Remove redundant `LangVersion` | In `Directory.Build.props` | Cleaner defaults |
| 5 | 🔵 | Add `global.json` | 1 new | Reproducible builds |

Combined, these changes would reduce the total lines of MSBuild XML across the 5 `.csproj` files from **~130 lines to ~45 lines**, while adding ~35 lines of well-structured central configuration. The net reduction in duplication is substantial, and more importantly, every setting has **exactly one authoritative source of truth**.

Would you like me to apply these fixes to the project files?

### E1: Package version drift — same package at different versions

`Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions` is referenced at **two different versions** across projects:

| `ContosoApp.Services` | **8.0.1** |
| `ContosoApp.Api` | **8.0.0** |

And the test frameworks are at divergent versions:

| Package | `Api.Tests` | `Core.Tests` |
|---|---|---|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.9.0** | **17.10.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.7.1** | **2.8.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.5.6** | **2.8.1** |

**Why it matters:** Version drift means different projects get different behaviors, bug fixes, and API surfaces. At build time NuGet will pick one version for the whole dependency graph, but the _intent_ is unclear — was `8.0.1` a deliberate upgrade that missed two projects, or an accidental bump? This class of bug gets worse as the solution grows.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see **S1** below) — it eliminates this entire category of problem.

## 🟡 Warnings (anti-patterns that should be fixed)

### W1: Massive duplication — 8 identical properties copy-pasted across ALL 5 projects

Every single `.csproj` repeats this block verbatim:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<LangVersion>latest</LangVersion>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

**Why it matters:** When you need to change the target framework to `net9.0`, or update the company name, you must touch all 5 files and hope nobody misses one. As the solution grows, this becomes untenable. A `Directory.Build.props` file is the standard solution (see **S2**).

All three `src/` projects contain an identical second `<PropertyGroup>`:
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>

This should be centralized. It also belongs in the same PropertyGroup as the other properties — there's no reason for it to be a separate block.

</Target>

This is a textbook case for `Directory.Build.targets`.

For SDK-style .NET 8 projects, the compiler automatically selects C# 12. Setting `LangVersion` to `latest` is equivalent to this default. More importantly, `latest` is a moving target — it silently changes meaning when you install a newer SDK. If you want the latest, the default is already that. If you want stability, pin to `12`.

**Recommendation:** Remove `LangVersion` entirely and rely on the SDK default, or pin to `12` if you want deterministic builds.
